title: Graded hopping screens nonreciprocity and reorganizes Stark asymptotics in a non-Hermitian Stark chain
authors: Y. S. Liu, X. Z. Zhang
abstract: We study a one-dimensional non-Hermitian Stark chain in which nonreciprocal hopping, a linear potential, and linearly graded hopping act simultaneously. The central question is how boundary pumping and field-induced confinement are reorganized when the hopping amplitude itself grows with position. We show that the graded term separates the two localization channels at the level of the large-position asymptotics. An exact diagonal similarity transformation removes the bond asymmetry and converts the usual exponential skin factor into an algebraic boundary accumulation with exponent $η=γ/F_2$. The transformed symmetric chain then reduces asymptotically to a constant-coefficient recurrence, giving the Stark threshold $|F_1|=2|F_2|$. The original right eigenstates acquire the unified envelope $ψ_j^R\sim j^ηφ_j$, with oscillatory, double-root, and exponentially localized branches across the threshold. This form also yields two finite-size scales, one measuring the logarithmic screening of nonreciprocity and the other balancing the algebraic skin factor against the exponential Stark tail. A joint localization map in the $(γ,F_1/F_2)$ plane verifies this structure. The edge polarization bends near the Stark threshold and weakens on the localized side, while the inverse participation ratio of the most localized eigenstates rises rapidly for $F_1/F_2>2$. Using a normalized Gaussian projector appropriate for non-unitary evolution, we further show that the same threshold enhances half-chain entanglement growth after a charge-density-wave quench. These results identify graded hopping as a controlled mechanism for screening nonreciprocity, resetting Stark asymptotics, and organizing the finite-size crossover between algebraic skin accumulation and Stark localization.
summary: This paper explores a 1D non-Hermitian chain where hopping strength increases with position. The researchers show that this graded hopping suppresses the typical exponential skin effect, replacing it with a power-law accumulation at the boundaries. They identify a specific threshold in the field strength where the system transitions between oscillatory and exponentially localized states. Furthermore, they demonstrate that this threshold also acts as a point of enhanced entanglement growth during many-body dynamics.
main_problem: The study investigates how graded hopping (position-dependent hopping) screens nonreciprocity and reorganizes the localization landscape and asymptotic behavior in a non-Hermitian Stark chain.
main_result: Graded hopping transforms the exponential non-Hermitian skin effect into a weaker algebraic boundary accumulation and identifies a critical threshold at |F1| = 2|F2| that regulates the transition between oscillatory and Stark-localized regimes.
method: The authors use an exact diagonal similarity transformation to remove bond asymmetry, asymptotic analysis of recurrence relations, and a QR-stabilized numerical approach for simulating non-unitary many-body dynamics.
model_or_system: A one-dimensional, open-boundary, non-Hermitian quadratic Hamiltonian (Stark chain) featuring nonreciprocal hopping, a linear potential (Stark field), and linearly graded hopping.
key_observables: Edge polarization, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), half-chain entanglement entropy, and center of mass.
important_parameters: Nonreciprocity strength (gamma), Stark slope (F1), hopping gradient (F2), and the critical threshold |F1| = 2|F2|.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be a quadratic (single-particle) fermionic model, and the orbital matrix must maintain full rank for the correlation matrix to be well-defined.
figures_summary: A joint localization map in the (gamma, F1/F2) plane showing edge polarization and IPR with specific line cuts to demonstrate phase transitions.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the non-Hermitian Stark chain model, applies a similarity transformation to analyze the asymptotic behavior of eigenstates, maps the localization regimes using one-body observables, and concludes with a many-body study of entanglement growth during a charge-density-wave quench.
why_interesting: It provides a controlled mechanism for screening nonreciprocity and reveals how non-Hermitian skin effects can be 'softened' into algebraic forms, which is highly relevant for understanding transport and entanglement in non-unitary many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Direct verification of the algebraic skin factor for N = 100, J = 1, F1 = 0, F2 = 1, and γ = 0.5, for which the analytical prediction is η = γ/F2 = 0.5. Panel (a) shows the exact diagonal similarity factor dj versus site index j on a log- log scale together with a power-law fit. In the displayed fitting window the fitted slope is ηfit ≃0.497, in good agreement with the analytical value 0.5. Panel (b) shows the local logarithmic increment ln(dj+1/dj) as a function of 1/j. A linear fit in the asymptotic window gives ln(dj+1/dj) ≃0.486/j, again very close to the expected coefficient η = 0.5. According to Eq. (26), its asymptotic slope is again fixed by η. The figure verifies that the...

FIG. 2. Joint localization map at fixed N = 100, J = 1, and F2 = 0.20. Panel (a) shows the mean edge polarization P of right eigenstates in the (γ, F1/F2) plane. Panel (b) shows the mean inverse participation ratio built from the most localized 20% of right eigenstates, IPR20%. The red dashed line marks the asymptotic Stark threshold F1/F2 = 2. The white dashed line marks the finite-size screening guide ΞN = (γ/F2) ln N = 5, corresponding to γ ≈0.219. Panels (c) and (d) show representative line cuts at γ = 0.081, 0.219, 0.362, and 0.481. Near the threshold the edge polarization displays a turning point, while the localization measure built from the most localized 20% develops a minimum and...

FIG. 3. Many-body entanglement growth from the half-filled charge-density-wave state of Eq. (15) in the Hermitian benchmark with N = 120, Np = 60, J = −1, γ = 0, and F2 = 0.08. Panel (a) shows the full time dependence of the half-chain entropy SN/2(t) for F1/F2 = 1, 2, and 3 in the interval 0 ≤t ≤8. Panel (b) enlarges the late-time interval t ∈[6, 8], where the threshold curve F1/F2 = 2 clearly lies above the other two. Panel (c) shows the excess entropy ∆S(t) defined in Eq. (51). The positive late-time excess entropy shows that the asymptotic Stark threshold leaves a direct dynamical trace in the growth of many-body entanglement.
